MODULE 6
SUMMARY NOTES
Your Instructor:
Mike Rhodes
What Is a Keyword?
Keywords are a word or a phrase in your account that links your ad to
search queries. For example, if someone searches for a word or phrase
similar to your keyword and Google believes it make sense to show them
your ad, they’ll see your ad on the search results page
Metrics of keyword performance are:
Cost Per Click (CPC): How much money you’re willing to give to Google if
someone clicks on your Ad with that given keyword
Interactions: How many people click on your Ad
Impressions: How many times your Ad has been shown
Tabs in Google AdWords (now Google Ads):
The Overview Tab: Provides a list of top campaigns, what’s been
changing, what days and hours are working best, etc.
The Opportunities Tab: Houses suggestions from the Google algorithm
The Campaigns Tab: A campaign is the main building block of your
Google Ads account. Inside of your account, you can have multiple
campaigns
The Ad Groups Tab: A campaign contains multiple Ad groups. Every Ad
group contains 2 elements: your keywords and your ads
The Ads and Extensions Tab: An extension is additional information
that can be added to your ad, like product information, phone number,
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

2
Module 6 Summary Notes
location, etc.
The Keywords Tab: This is where you’ll find all of the keywords you are
using in an ad set and how those keywords are performing
The Settings Tab: The main settings for your particular campaign
The Locations Tab: You can select the geographic location of the Google
users you are targeting with your ad
The Ad Schedule: Allows you to choose the time of day that your ads
are shown to Google users and to adjust your bid on specific days and at
specific times
The Devices Tab: Allows you to choose what devices you want your ad to
be shown on (desktop, computer, tablet, etc.)
The Change History Tab: You can see the history of all budget changes
made to every ad in your Google Ads account
Knowing Your Business and Marketing Goals
A Google Ads marketing strategy can only work well if you have identified
what goal you are trying to achieve
After you have defined your goal, create MTO targets that define the
spectrum of customer acquisition. MTO stands for minimum, target, and
optimal:
• Minimum- The minimum acceptable amount that you’ll acquire a
customer for
• Target- The target number that you want to spend to acquire a
customer
• Optimal- The best price that you can acquire a customer for
Your Key Performance Indicator (KPIs) is the one metric that you define as
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 8 --

3
Module 6 Summary Notes
the number that matters the most in your Ads strategy. The easiest KPI is
cost per lead or cost per sale
Keywords vs. Search Terms
The keyword acts like a magnet that attracts search terms similar to it
Search terms are what the Google user types into the search engine
Pay attention to your visitor’s search words to see how you can improve
your keywords
Keyword Match Types:
• Exact: Google only matches your ad to a search query of your exact
keywords
• Phrase: Google matches your ad to users with a search query that
contains your phrase but there can also be other words before or after
(not in between)
• Broad: Google matches your ad to users searching for the exact
keyword and broadly related searches
Advanced Keyword Match Types:
• Modified Broad: By adding plus signs to the front of your keywords,
Google matches your keyword(s) and the search query on a spectrum
between exact phrase and broad
• Negative: Based off of keywords of your choice, Google won’t show
your ad to search queries containing specific words
• Close Variant: Google will show ads for search queries are very close
to your keyword
The number one strategy for Google Ads is to continuously test ads
against each other
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

4
Module 6 Summary Notes
What elements of ads should you test?
• Headlines
• Call to Actions (CTAs)
• Benefits
• Paths
Examples of Action Words to include in your ad copy are:
Go, buy, get, tap, beat, feel, lift, open, pick, save, shop, talk, apply, begin,
order, score, decide, enroll, redeem, select, compare, imagine, uncover,
increase, customize, etc.
You must be aware of Google’s AdWords policies and it’s good practice
to always stay up to date. In particular, pay attention to the editorial
guidelines. You can see them here: goo.gl/5mUQXU
A rule of thumb: If you can’t fit all of your keywords into the ad copy, you
probably have too many keywords
Building Campaigns
There are various reasons to have more than one campaign. For example:
• Differing types of campaigns (banners, search network, shopping ads,
etc.)
• Differing campaigns for locations (zip codes, states, countries)
• Differing products campaigns for specific products over your business
as a whole
• Different CPAs depending on how much each customer is worth
acquiring based off of the product
Conversion Tracking
A conversion happens when a user takes the action that your ad was
designed to motivate them to take
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

5
Module 6 Summary Notes
Ongoing Optimization
The structure and tracking of your Adwords campaigns rarely changes.
The elements of the campaign that are regularly optimized are:
• Changing bids
• Adding new keywords
• Testing ad copy
Quality Score: An automated opinion of how Google “thinks” your ad
will perform. Remember, profit is the number one metric of success. The
quality score is just a guide that showcases the potential of an ad
Bidding
There are 2 types of bidding, manual and automated bidding
At the beginning of your Google Ads strategy, use manual bidding and
later you can integrate automated bidding
Types of Automated Bidding
• Enhanced CPC: Google changes the bid based off of how likely the
user is to convert
• Target CPA: You tell Google what your target is and then Google
chooses what user to show an ad to and what users not to show an ad
to—this is best used after getting 100 conversions a month
• Target Return on Ad Spend (ROAS): Used by retailers and
ecommerce stores that need to make a specific amount of money back
from each click-best used by retailers selling a lot of items
Ad Extensions
Ad Extensions: Extra information that can be added to your ad and they
look like this:
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 8 --

6
Module 6 Summary Notes
Google favors ads that have ad extensions
Benefits of Ad Extensions:
• Increases the amount of benefits you can place on your ad
• Makes your ad bigger, pushing organic results further down the page
• Extensions are part of the Ad Rank Formula, so using them increases
your ad rank
Examples of Ad Extensions include:
• Sitelinks
• Callouts
• Structured Snippets
• Call
• Location
• Prices
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 8 --

7
Module 6 Summary Notes
Remarketing
Remarketing (or retargeting): When you tag users that come to your
website and follow them while they “travel” around the rest of the web
To create the tag, go to “Audience List” and then click on “Website
Visitors.” You’ll receive a piece of code that goes on to your website to
retarget users
This gives you the ability to build a simple audience of users who have
visited specific pages of your website and show them ads when they visit
other websites
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
